original_abstract: The design and additive manufacturing of metamaterials with tailored architectures allow for the realization of unprecedented mechanical properties that surpass those of their individual constituents. Traditionally, these properties are characterized by response curves, such as the stress-strain profiles that define a material’s quasi-static signature. Despite recent progress, current inverse design frameworks struggle to replicate specific, complex behaviors due to the challenges of multi-objective optimization, inherent non-linearity, and the prevalence of process-dependent manufacturing defects. In this study, we present an accelerated inverse design methodology that integrates generative machine learning with desktop additive manufacturing to synthesize nearly any uniaxial compressive stress-strain profile while accounting for structural deviations introduced during printing. Our findings demonstrate that full mechanical tailorability is achievable, maintaining nearly 90% fidelity between predicted targets and experimental measurements. By streamlining the path from prescribed behavior to physical realization, this approach provides a robust framework for engineering advanced materials while bypassing traditional, time-consuming iterative design-manufacturing cycles.
humanized_abstract: Design and additive manufacturing of metamaterials with tailored microstructure offers unprecedented opportunities to tailor new mechanical properties. These are often described through the conventional stress–strain response, i.e., the material’s quasi-static behaviour. While significant advances have been reported, existing inverse design methods are limited in terms of ability to tailor complex and multi-functional behaviours, due to the difficulties in achieving multi-objective optimization, non-linearity, and process-dependent defects, which are inherent to the additive manufacturing process. In this work, we develop a fast inverse design strategy that leverages generative machine learning and the corresponding desktop additive manufacturing technology to design and 3D print nearly any uniaxial compressive stress–strain profile, while accommodating the printing-induced structural defects. Our results show that full mechanical tailoring is feasible with less than 10% loss of target performance and nearly 90% measurement consistency. Our method thus enables a rapid transition from desired mechanical behaviour to material realization, eliminating the need for lengthy trial-and-error design and manufacturing iterations.

Our method thus enables a rapid transition from desired mechanical behaviour to material realization, eliminating the need for lengthy trial-and-error design and manufacturing iterations.
